next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By the time the B44 finally wheezed to the curb on Church Avenue, Marisol had gone from cold to damp and back to cold again. Slush soaked the edge of her jeans where it licked over her boots, and the paper bag with the clementines had started to sag at one corner. She shifted it to her other hand, felt the hard coin shape of the blue chip in her coat pocket, and looked up at Carina's building like she had to relearn where the front door was.

Carina had texted at seven in the morning.

If you're coming, come by ten. Leo has math and catechism worksheet. Don't be late.

There was no emoji and no sister-softener, just logistics, which for Carina counted as mercy.

She had mapped the morning by the hour before she slept and still landed two minutes late.

A man in a Mets cap let her into the vestibule and held the second door with his shoulder while balancing two laundry baskets. The hallway smelled like bleach over old cooking oil, and then, one floor up, like coffee and candle wax and sofrito that had settled deep into plaster over fifteen years of dinners. Marisol paused outside 3B with her fist lifted, not because she forgot how to knock, but because her body remembered the last time she stood here in muscle and skin before her head caught up. She remembered the table with documents spread out, Carina's hand on the suitcase handle, and Leo standing in the doorway in his dinosaur pajamas, crying without sound.

She knocked anyway.

Carina opened fast, as if she had been waiting behind the door and did not want to look like she had been waiting. She had her hai...
open_hooks_to_carry: Writing process foregrounds revision choices rather than inspiration myth.
Luis challenges Marisol to distinguish exposure from amends.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ase, sped up when she did not raise a hand fast enough.

She called Mr. Han, the Sunset Park landlord. Straight to voicemail. Twenty seconds later his auto-text hit: `Rent past due. Pay $400 tonight or lock changes 10:00 a.m.`

She typed to Ada: `I got put out. Any chance I can crash on your couch tonight?` The message flipped to delivered and sat there. No typing bubbles. No reply.

The bus shelter at the corner had one panel cracked and taped in an X. She rolled the suitcase in, sat on the bench, and pulled her scarf up over her mouth. Two teenagers in puffer coats shared earbuds and laughed at something on a phone. A woman in scrubs counted singles from her wallet twice, then a third time, lips moving with each number.

Marisol took the folder out and checked the pages again by streetlight, as if one might have changed while she was upstairs.

Witness: Salvador Rivas.

Cause: acute combined fentanyl and heroin toxicity.

Room 214.

Facts sat still. People did not.

The bus app said the next B41 southbound would arrive in twelve minutes, then updated to fourteen, then eleven, algorithmic mercy. Marisol opened Notes and typed three words: `No stable housing.` She stared at them until they blurred and added a fourth: `Tonight.`

She put the phone down and pressed thumb and forefinger against the bridge of her nose until stars burst in her vision. She was not drunk. She was not safe. She was not done.

When the bus finally came, she stood, lifted the crooked suitcase, and climbed on, heading south with Mr. Han’s deadline ticking and no clean plan for where she would get off.
state_deltas_required: Draft a catalogue essay before Salvador dies and the record hardens without her.
She commits to a documentary-poetic draft that includes her relapse and refuses to cast any single person as pure martyr or villain.
